

**![][image1]**

Feature Proposal Document

Next-Phase Development Roadmap

Platform: Secure Legal Document Management System

Stack: Hyperledger Fabric · IPFS · Spring Boot · React 18 · WebCrypto API

Classification: Internal Development Roadmap

**CONFIDENTIAL**

# **Section 1 — Executive Summary**

PangoChain is a secure legal document management platform designed for law firms and their clients. It addresses a fundamental and long-standing weakness in legal practice: the fact that sensitive case documents — contracts, evidence, confessions, medical records — are routinely handled via general-purpose tools such as email attachments and cloud file shares, which provide no cryptographic guarantee of confidentiality, integrity, or non-repudiation.

The platform combines three complementary technologies. Hyperledger Fabric, a permissioned enterprise blockchain, provides an immutable, independently verifiable audit ledger for every case event and document operation. IPFS, the InterPlanetary File System, provides decentralised, content-addressed storage that ensures documents cannot be silently deleted or altered after submission. The browser-native WebCrypto API provides end-to-end encryption, ensuring that plaintext document content never leaves the client device — the server stores only ciphertext, and only a holder of the appropriate private key can read a document.

In its current form, PangoChain supports two user communities: legal professionals (including managing partners, associates, paralegals, IT administrators, and regulators) and their clients. Legal professionals can create and manage cases, upload and categorise encrypted documents, schedule and manage hearings, communicate via end-to-end encrypted messaging, manage team access to documents with time-bounded permission grants, and review a comprehensive, blockchain-anchored audit trail. Clients have access to a secure portal showing their case status, upcoming hearings, shared documents, and private messaging to their legal team. Both communities benefit from an identical cryptographic stack: AES-256-GCM document encryption, ECDSA P-256 digital signatures, ECIES P-256 key wrapping, and PBKDF2-SHA256 key derivation at 600,000 iterations.

This Feature Proposal Document defines the next phase of PangoChain's development. It catalogues the existing feature set in full, proposes twenty-seven new capabilities across the lawyer and client sides of the platform, describes the architectural implications of each major addition, and establishes a prioritised development roadmap. The proposed features range from immediately deliverable quality-of-life improvements — such as a document version history interface and TOTP recovery codes — to ambitious, research-grade innovations including a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) legal AI assistant, a multi-party blockchain-anchored digital signature workflow, and a cross-firm secure data room. Taken together, they position PangoChain as a genuinely differentiated legal technology platform capable of competing with established enterprise solutions while remaining grounded in demonstrable cryptographic and distributed systems research.

# **Section 2 — Current Feature Inventory**

## **2.1  Lawyer / Legal Professional Side**

The following table enumerates every existing feature on the legal professional side of the platform, together with a concise description of its technical implementation and the user-facing value it delivers.

| Feature | Technical Implementation | User-Facing Value |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **JWT Authentication** | JWT access \+ refresh tokens with PBKDF2-SHA256 password hashing. | Secure, stateless login across all sessions. |
| **TOTP Multi-Factor Auth** | TOTP MFA enforced for Managing Partner and IT Admin roles. | Prevents account takeover on privileged accounts. |
| **ECDSA P-256 Signing Keys** | Keypair generated at registration; private key AES-GCM wrapped under PBKDF2 key in localStorage. | Enables legally attributable document signatures. |
| **ECIES P-256 Encryption Keys** | Encryption keypair generated at registration; private key wrapped identically to signing key. | Per-user key wrapping for document access grants. |
| **Case Creation & Management** | Cases registered on Hyperledger Fabric; CRUD backed by PostgreSQL. | Full lifecycle management of legal matters on an immutable ledger. |
| **Case Detail — Documents Tab** | Document list with encrypt/download pipeline and category filters. | Centralised document workspace per case. |
| **Case Detail — Hearings Tab** | Hearings CRUD with court, location, datetime, and notes. | Scheduling within the case context. |
| **Case Detail — Team Access** | TeamAccessPanel: ECIES-wrapped key grants (owner/write/read) with optional expiry. | Granular, time-bounded access control. |
| **Case Detail — Timeline Tab** | Paginated ledger event feed fetched via GetHistoryForKey. | Immutable chronological audit of case activity. |
| **Document Encryption Upload** | Browser-side AES-256-GCM encryption; IV prepended to ciphertext; pushed to IPFS. | Plaintext never leaves the client device. |
| **Document SHA-256 Anchoring** | Plaintext SHA-256 hash written to Fabric ledger at upload time. | Tamper-evident integrity proof independent of storage. |
| **Secure Document Download** | IPFS fetch → ECIES unwrap → AES-GCM decrypt → SHA-256 verify. | Four-stage cryptographic integrity check on every access. |
| **Document Categories** | Seven categories: GENERAL, EVIDENCE, CONTRACT, CORRESPONDENCE, CONFESSION, MEDICAL, FINANCIAL. | Organised retrieval and compliance tagging. |
| **Confidential Flag** | Per-document flag with red badge UI; enforced at ACL layer. | Visual and technical enforcement of sensitivity levels. |
| **Document Versioning (DB)** | Previous version chain stored in PostgreSQL (previous\_version\_id). | Preserves full document history at the data layer. |
| **Two-Layer ACL** | Spring Security JWT RBAC (Layer 1\) \+ CheckAccess chaincode at query time (Layer 2). | Defense-in-depth: server and blockchain both enforce access. |
| **ACL Fallback Logging** | Graceful degradation to DB ACL when Fabric unreachable, logged as ACL\_FABRIC\_FALLBACK. | Continuity of service without silent security bypass. |
| **Dual-Store Audit Log** | Every event written to Fabric ledger and PostgreSQL append-only audit\_log (INSERT-only trigger). | Cryptographic audit trail with database backup. |
| **Ledger Explorer** | Browse blockchain events with Fabric transaction IDs via REST. | Transparency and verifiability for compliance officers. |
| **Audit Trail Page** | Paginated, filterable audit log UI by event type and resource ID. | Practical audit review for lawyers and IT admins. |
| **E2E Encrypted Messaging** | AES-256-GCM \+ ECIES P-256; plaintext never stored; threads by sender/recipient. | Privileged communications with cryptographic confidentiality. |
| **Hearing Management** | Schedule, list, and send high-priority reminders; calendar-style date badges. | Court deadline management with client notification. |
| **Role-Aware Dashboard** | Counts of active cases, documents, next hearing, unread messages; adapts per role. | At-a-glance operational overview. |
| **User Administration** | Activate / suspend users; display public key fingerprints. | Firm-level identity governance. |

## **2.2  Client Portal Side**

The following table enumerates every existing feature on the client side of the platform.

| Feature | Technical Implementation | User-Facing Value |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Welcome Dashboard** | Encryption status badge, next hearing countdown, stats row (docs, messages, reminders, audits). | Immediate situational awareness on login. |
| **Reminders Panel** | View lawyer-sent reminders; mark as read; HIGH priority badge. | Never miss a critical instruction from counsel. |
| **Quick Action Cards** | Upload, Message, Document Vault, Case Timeline shortcuts. | One-tap navigation to key workflows. |
| **Privacy Notice Panel** | Displays live crypto spec: AES-256-GCM, ECIES P-256, PBKDF2 iterations. | Builds trust by showing clients exactly how their data is protected. |
| **Document Vault** | Category filter tabs; inline upload with category \+ confidential flag; full encrypt pipeline. | Self-service document management with the same security as the lawyer side. |
| **SecureDownloadModal** | Four-stage progress UI: Fetch → Unwrap → Decrypt → Verify. | Transparent, reassuring visualisation of cryptographic operations. |
| **CONFIDENTIAL Badge & Styling** | Red badge and distinct styling for flagged documents. | Immediate visual cue for sensitive materials. |
| **My Case — Hearings View** | Upcoming hearings with past/today/future colour coding. | Clear, colour-coded view of court obligations. |
| **Privacy Rights Panel** | Five-point GDPR-aligned checklist of client data rights. | Regulatory transparency and informed consent. |
| **Blockchain Audit Timeline** | Client-visible event feed with Fabric transaction IDs. | Proof that the platform cannot silently alter records. |
| **E2E Client Messaging** | Same encrypted messaging stack; client can message any user in their firm. | Secure channel to counsel without email exposure. |

# **Section 3 — Proposed New Features: Lawyer / Legal Professional Side**

The following seventeen features are proposed for the lawyer and legal professional side of the platform. Each is presented with a problem statement, user experience description, technical implementation sketch, wow-factor rating, complexity assessment, and dependency on existing features.

| 1\. RAG-Powered Legal AI Assistant |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Lawyers spend excessive time manually reviewing case files to answer routine questions. |
| **User Experience** | A persistent chat panel on the Case Detail page allows the lawyer to ask natural-language questions — 'What did the opposing party admit in Exhibit C?' — and receive answers grounded in the actual uploaded case documents, with source citations linking back to the relevant file. |
| **Technical Sketch** | React chat component POSTs to a Spring Boot /ai/query endpoint. The backend decrypts nothing; instead, the client sends the plaintext of documents it has already decrypted locally (browser-to-server over TLS, session-scoped). A LangChain4j service chunks the text, embeds with a local sentence-transformer (pgvector in PostgreSQL), performs semantic retrieval, and passes context to an LLM (OpenAI GPT-4o or locally hosted Mistral). No plaintext is persisted server-side. |
| **Dependencies** | Secure Document Download, Case Document List, PostgreSQL (pgvector extension) |

| 2\. Smart Contract Template Engine |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Drafting standard legal documents is repetitive and error-prone when done manually. |
| **User Experience** | A template library page lists parameterised document templates (NDA, Retainer, Settlement). The lawyer fills a guided form with case-specific values; the system renders a preview, encrypts the generated document, and anchors a 'template version \+ parameter hash' record on the Fabric ledger, locking the exact template version used against the case. |
| **Technical Sketch** | React form-builder with Handlebars-style variable substitution. Spring Boot /templates endpoint with versioned template records in PostgreSQL. New chaincode function RecordTemplateGeneration stores template ID, parameter hash, and case ID on the ledger. Generated document follows the full AES-256-GCM upload pipeline. |
| **Dependencies** | Document Encryption Upload, Case Management, Fabric Chaincode |

| 3\. AI Document Classification & Auto-Tagging |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Uploaded documents are often miscategorised or untagged, degrading searchability. |
| **User Experience** | On upload, a classification badge appears with a suggested category and confidence score. The lawyer can accept or override with one click. Over time, firm-specific feedback trains a custom classifier. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot intercepts the upload metadata (filename, size, user-supplied plaintext-side preview). A fine-tuned text classification model (served via Python FastAPI sidecar) returns a category prediction. The suggestion is stored in a document\_classification\_log table; accepted suggestions trigger an UPDATE to the document category with an audit entry. No ciphertext is ever sent to the classifier. |
| **Dependencies** | Document Encryption Upload, Document Categories |

| 4\. Audit Anomaly Detection Engine |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Unusual or potentially malicious document access patterns are invisible until damage is done. |
| **User Experience** | A Security Alerts section on the Admin Dashboard highlights anomalous events — e.g., 'User X accessed 47 documents between 02:00–03:00,' or 'Document Y accessed 12 times in 10 minutes.' Each alert links to the full audit trail entry with Fabric tx ID. |
| **Technical Sketch** | A Spring Boot scheduled task runs statistical analysis (Z-score on access frequency) over the PostgreSQL audit\_log nightly. Alerts are written to a security\_alert table with severity and description. The Admin React page polls /security/alerts. Fabric tx IDs in alerts allow independent verification. An ACL\_ANOMALY\_DETECTED audit event is written to both stores. |
| **Dependencies** | Dual-Store Audit Log, Ledger Explorer, User Administration |

| 5\. Deadline & Statute of Limitations Tracker |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Missing a legal deadline can constitute professional negligence with severe consequences. |
| **User Experience** | A Deadlines panel on the Case Detail page lists all tracked dates with colour-coded urgency (red \< 7 days, amber \< 30 days, green \> 30 days). Lawyers add custom deadlines and link them to document evidence. The system automatically calculates statute of limitations dates from configurable jurisdiction rules. |
| **Technical Sketch** | React deadline widget backed by a case\_deadline table (Liquibase migration). Spring Boot /cases/{id}/deadlines CRUD endpoint with @Scheduled email reminders using JavaMailSender. Jurisdiction rules stored as JSON in a jurisdiction\_rules table. Deadline creation anchored on Fabric with RecordDeadline chaincode function. |
| **Dependencies** | Case Management, Hearing Management, Fabric Chaincode |

| 6\. Cross-Firm Secure Data Room |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Sharing discovery documents with opposing counsel currently requires insecure email or third-party services. |
| **User Experience** | A senior lawyer creates a Data Room from a case, selects documents and sets an expiry date. An invitation link is sent to the external party. The external user authenticates via a time-limited token, and can view (not download) documents within a sandboxed viewer. All access is audited. |
| **Technical Sketch** | New data\_room and data\_room\_invite tables with Liquibase migration. Documents shared to a Data Room have ECIES keys re-wrapped under a Data Room symmetric key, itself wrapped under the invitee's public key (or a session key derived from the invitation token). Spring Boot /data-rooms endpoints. New Fabric chaincode function RecordDataRoomAccess. External access logs written to both audit stores. |
| **Dependencies** | Two-Layer ACL, Document Encryption Upload, Team Access Panel, E2E Messaging |

| 7\. Multi-Party Digital Signature Workflow |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Executing agreements requires a legally attributable, tamper-evident signing ceremony that email-based workflows cannot provide. |
| **User Experience** | The lawyer initiates a signing workflow on any document, specifies signatories (internal or external) and signing order. Each signatory receives a notification, reviews the document, and applies their ECDSA P-256 signature. On completion, the system generates a blockchain-anchored Signing Certificate PDF listing all signatures, timestamps, and Fabric tx IDs. |
| **Technical Sketch** | signing\_workflow, signing\_request, and signature tables. Each browser-side ECDSA signature is produced over SHA-256(document\_hash \+ workflow\_id \+ signer\_id) using the WebCrypto API. Spring Boot orchestrates the workflow state machine. Final Fabric chaincode call RecordSigningCompletion anchors the aggregate signature proof. The certificate is a server-generated PDF with QR code linking to the Fabric tx. |
| **Dependencies** | ECDSA Signing Keys, Document SHA-256 Anchoring, Fabric Chaincode, Dual-Store Audit |

| 8\. Automated Compliance Report Generator |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Preparing GDPR compliance and bar association reports is manual, time-intensive, and inconsistent. |
| **User Experience** | From the Admin Dashboard, an IT Admin selects a report type (GDPR Data Inventory, Access Log Summary, Data Breach Readiness) and date range, clicks Generate, and downloads a branded PDF in under 30 seconds. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot /reports endpoint queries PostgreSQL audit\_log and user/document tables, aggregates data, and renders a PDF using Apache FOP (XSL-FO templates). No Fabric queries required for standard reports; the Fabric tx ID column in audit\_log provides on-demand verification links. Report generation events are themselves audit-logged. |
| **Dependencies** | Dual-Store Audit Log, User Administration, Audit Trail Page |

| 9\. Client-Side Document Redaction Tool |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Producing redacted versions of sensitive documents for disclosure requires a secure, auditable workflow. |
| **User Experience** | A Redaction Editor opens a document in a browser-native PDF/text renderer. The lawyer draws black rectangles over text regions. On save, the client-side code produces a redacted copy, encrypts it separately, and uploads it to IPFS with a new CID. Both the original and redacted versions are linked in the DB with an explicit redaction\_of relationship. |
| **Technical Sketch** | React-based PDF.js viewer with canvas overlay for redaction mask drawing. The final redacted plaintext is reconstructed in-browser (never server-side), encrypted with a fresh AES-256-GCM key, and uploaded following the existing IPFS pipeline. A document\_redaction table records the parent–child CID pair. Fabric chaincode function RecordRedaction anchors both CIDs. |
| **Dependencies** | Document Encryption Upload, Secure Document Download, Two-Layer ACL, Fabric Chaincode |

| 10\. Chain of Custody Visualiser |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Proving document provenance and access history in court requires a readable, irrefutable visual record. |
| **User Experience** | A document's history page renders an interactive directed acyclic graph showing upload, every access event, each version creation, all sharing grants, and every signature — each node linked to a Fabric tx ID. Clicking a node opens the raw audit record. The graph is exportable as a court-ready PDF. |
| **Technical Sketch** | React component using D3.js to render the custody graph. Data sourced from the Spring Boot /documents/{id}/custody endpoint, which queries both Fabric (GetHistoryForKey) and PostgreSQL audit\_log, merges the two timelines, and returns a structured JSON graph. PDF export via server-side PDF generation with the same graph data. |
| **Dependencies** | Dual-Store Audit Log, Ledger Explorer, Document Versioning, Digital Signature Workflow |

| 11\. Predictive Case Outcome Analysis |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Lawyers have no structured data-driven tool to set realistic client expectations on case outcomes. |
| **User Experience** | A Case Insights panel shows predicted outcome probability distributions (e.g., 'Based on 240 similar anonymised cases: 68% settlement, 21% claimant win, 11% defendant win') with confidence intervals and the key factors driving the prediction. |
| **Technical Sketch** | An offline ML pipeline (scikit-learn / XGBoost) trains on anonymised, aggregated case metadata (jurisdiction, case type, document count, duration) stored in a case\_analytics\_view. The trained model is serialised and served via a Python FastAPI inference endpoint. Spring Boot proxies the prediction request. Critically, no document content is ever sent to the model — only structured metadata. |
| **Dependencies** | Case Management, Document Versioning (DB), Hearing Management |

| 12\. Billing Matter Integration & Time Tracking |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Billable hours linked to specific documents and case activities are tracked in external systems, creating reconciliation overhead. |
| **User Experience** | A Time Tracker widget floats on every case page. Lawyers start/stop timers per task, link the entry to a document or hearing, and add a description. A Billing Summary page shows accrued hours per matter. Client-facing invoices are generated in one click. |
| **Technical Sketch** | billing\_matter, time\_entry, and invoice tables (the billing\_matter table already exists in the DB schema). Spring Boot /billing endpoints with CRUD and @PreAuthorize role gating. Invoice PDF generation via Apache FOP. Time entry creation writes an audit event. The existing document and case foreign keys are used to link time entries. |
| **Dependencies** | Case Management, Document Management, Dual-Store Audit Log, User Administration |

| 13\. Conflict of Interest Checker |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Representing parties with conflicting interests — even unknowingly — exposes firms to disciplinary action. |
| **User Experience** | When a new case is created, the system automatically scans all active and closed cases for the same or related party names and surfaces a Conflict Warning with a list of matching cases. The lawyer must explicitly acknowledge or override the warning before the case is created. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot /cases/conflict-check endpoint performs fuzzy name matching (Levenshtein distance) over the client\_name, opposing\_party\_name, and related\_parties columns across all cases. A conflict\_check\_log table records every check with the result, timestamp, and acknowledging user. The case creation Fabric transaction includes a conflict\_check\_acknowledged field. |
| **Dependencies** | Case Management, Dual-Store Audit Log |

| 14\. Court-Ready PDF Bundle Generator |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Assembling discovery bundles for court filing is labour-intensive and risks omitting critical integrity proofs. |
| **User Experience** | The lawyer selects documents from a case, chooses a bundle template (Discovery Bundle, Evidence Bundle), and clicks Generate. The system produces a single PDF with a cover page, table of contents, page-numbered documents, and a final Blockchain Integrity Appendix listing each document's SHA-256 hash and Fabric tx ID. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot /bundles endpoint. Decryption of selected documents happens client-side (browser downloads all, produces plaintext array). The client POSTs the plaintext array (TLS-only, session-scoped) to a bundle generation endpoint which assembles PDF pages using Apache PDFBox. The Integrity Appendix is populated by querying the Fabric ledger for each document's hash record. |
| **Dependencies** | Secure Document Download, Document SHA-256 Anchoring, Fabric Chaincode, Audit Trail Page |

| 15\. Real-Time Collaborative Document Annotation |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Lawyers on the same case cannot comment on shared documents without reverting to email threads. |
| **User Experience** | Clicking 'Annotate' on a document opens an in-browser viewer with a margin comment panel. Any authorised team member on the case can add, reply to, or resolve comments. All annotations are visible in real time via WebSocket. Each annotation is linked to a specific document version hash, so comments remain anchored even as new versions are uploaded. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot WebSocket endpoint (/ws/annotations) with STOMP protocol. Annotations stored in a document\_annotation table with document\_id, version\_hash, position, and author. WebSocket messages are signed with the author's ECDSA key so recipients can verify authorship client-side. This feature also resolves the known gap of the missing WebSocket/SSE implementation. |
| **Dependencies** | Document Versioning (DB), ECDSA Signing Keys, Team Access Panel, Two-Layer ACL |

| 16\. TOTP Recovery Code System |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Users locked out of TOTP MFA with no recovery path must contact IT Admin, creating operational disruption. |
| **User Experience** | At MFA enrolment, the user is shown ten single-use recovery codes to print or save securely. Entering a recovery code at the login screen bypasses TOTP for a single session and immediately prompts re-enrolment. Each code can only be used once. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot generates 10 PBKDF2-hashed recovery codes stored in a user\_recovery\_code table (hashed, not plaintext). The login endpoint checks submitted codes against hashed values, invalidates on match, and forces MFA re-enrolment on next session. Recovery code usage is audit-logged to both PostgreSQL and Fabric. |
| **Dependencies** | TOTP MFA, JWT Authentication, Dual-Store Audit Log |

| 17\. Document Version History UI |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | The DB-level document version chain has no frontend, leaving lawyers unable to browse or restore previous versions. |
| **User Experience** | A Version History panel on the Document Detail page lists all prior versions with upload timestamp, uploader name, and a Restore button. Restoring a version creates a new version linked to the selected prior, preserving the entire chain. A diff view highlights changes between any two versions. |
| **Technical Sketch** | React VersionHistoryPanel component queries the existing /documents/{id}/versions endpoint (to be built) which traverses the previous\_version\_id chain in PostgreSQL. Restore creates a new document record with the previous version's IPFS CID but a new Fabric anchor. Diff view uses a browser-side diff library (diff-match-patch) on the client-decrypted plaintexts. |
| **Dependencies** | Document Versioning (DB), Secure Document Download, Document Encryption Upload |

# **Section 4 — Proposed New Features: Client Side**

The following ten features are proposed for the client portal side of the platform. They are designed to improve transparency, trust, and ease of access for clients navigating what is often a stressful and unfamiliar process.

| 1\. AI Case Assistant (Client-Facing Chatbot) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Clients are often confused about their case status and cannot access their lawyer immediately. |
| **User Experience** | A chat icon on the client dashboard opens an AI assistant that answers questions in plain, non-legal language: 'When is my next hearing?' 'What documents have been uploaded this week?' 'What does Exhibit B say about the incident date?' Answers are grounded in the client's own documents and case data. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Same RAG architecture as the lawyer-side assistant, but scoped strictly to documents the client has been granted access to (enforced via the existing two-layer ACL before any plaintext is passed to the LLM context). A client\_ai\_query\_log table records every query with timestamp and user ID for compliance. The LLM response is streamed to the React chat component via Server-Sent Events. |
| **Dependencies** | Two-Layer ACL, Secure Document Download, RAG-Powered Legal AI Assistant (Lawyer Feature) |

| 2\. Case Progress Timeline with Milestones |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Clients have no visibility into where their case stands relative to expected resolution, causing anxiety and repeated enquiries to counsel. |
| **User Experience** | A visual timeline on the My Case page shows completed, in-progress, and upcoming milestones — Intake, Discovery, Pre-Trial Motions, Hearing, Resolution — with estimated dates sourced from the hearing scheduler and lawyer-set milestones. The current position is highlighted. |
| **Technical Sketch** | React timeline component backed by a case\_milestone table. Spring Boot /cases/{id}/milestones CRUD endpoint gated to lawyer write / client read. Milestone creation and completion anchored on Fabric with RecordMilestone chaincode function. Estimated completion calculated from hearing dates and average milestone durations in similar case types. |
| **Dependencies** | Case Management, Hearing Management, Fabric Chaincode |

| 3\. Secure Video Consultation Booking |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Scheduling and conducting video consultations requires switching between PangoChain and external tools, breaking the secure workflow. |
| **User Experience** | The client clicks Book a Consultation, selects an available slot from the lawyer's published availability, and confirms. At the scheduled time, a Join Meeting button appears. The video call is conducted in an embedded WebRTC session using the browser's media stack, with the session key exchanged via the ECIES infrastructure already in place. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot /consultations endpoint with availability\_slot and consultation\_booking tables. WebRTC peer connection established via a Spring Boot TURN/STUN proxy. Session encryption keys derived from ECIES key exchange between the two participants' existing key pairs. Booking confirmed to both parties via the existing hearing reminder email pathway. Booking recorded on Fabric with RecordConsultation. |
| **Dependencies** | Hearing Management, ECIES P-256 Encryption Keys, E2E Messaging |

| 4\. Client-Side Document Signing |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Clients must currently sign documents outside the platform, breaking the chain of custody. |
| **User Experience** | A Sign Document button appears on documents flagged for client signature. The client reviews the document, types their name as intent confirmation, and clicks Sign. An ECDSA P-256 signature is produced in the browser using the client's existing key. The signature is displayed with a timestamp and Fabric tx ID for immediate verification. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Uses the existing WebCrypto ECDSA P-256 signing stack already deployed on the client side. The browser signs SHA-256(document\_hash \+ case\_id \+ timestamp). Spring Boot /documents/{id}/sign endpoint records the signature in the document\_signature table and writes a RecordClientSignature Fabric transaction. The signature can be independently verified against the client's public key stored in the platform. |
| **Dependencies** | ECDSA P-256 Signing Keys, Document SHA-256 Anchoring, Two-Layer ACL, Fabric Chaincode |

| 5\. Settlement Offer Comparison Tool |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Clients struggle to compare multiple settlement offers without structured analytical support. |
| **User Experience** | The lawyer adds structured settlement offers to the case. The client sees a side-by-side comparison table with monetary values, non-monetary terms, and a pros/cons analysis generated by the AI assistant. The client can mark an offer as Accepted or Rejected, which triggers a workflow notification to the lawyer. |
| **Technical Sketch** | settlement\_offer table with structured JSON fields. React comparison component renders offer cards side by side. The AI analysis is generated via the same LLM endpoint as the case assistant, prompted with the offer fields and case summary. Client acceptance/rejection recorded in the DB and anchored on Fabric with RecordSettlementResponse. |
| **Dependencies** | Case Management, AI Case Assistant, Fabric Chaincode |

| 6\. Client Satisfaction & Feedback System |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Firms have no structured mechanism to collect client feedback on service quality after key events. |
| **User Experience** | After each hearing and after case closure, the client receives a prompt to complete a short satisfaction survey (1–5 stars \+ free-text). Aggregate ratings are visible to the Managing Partner on the Admin Dashboard. Individual responses are shared with the relevant lawyer. |
| **Technical Sketch** | feedback\_prompt and feedback\_response tables. Spring Boot @Scheduled event triggers feedback prompts after hearing\_datetime passes or case status transitions to CLOSED. React survey modal displayed on next client login. Responses stored with client\_id, case\_id, event\_id. Aggregate scores computed by the /analytics/satisfaction endpoint. |
| **Dependencies** | Hearing Management, Case Management, Role-Aware Dashboard |

| 7\. Expense & Invoice Transparency Portal |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Clients have no visibility into accrued billing, leading to invoice disputes and trust erosion. |
| **User Experience** | A Billing tab on the client My Case page shows itemised time entries (date, description, duration, rate) linked to the documents or hearings they relate to. Unpaid invoices display a status badge. Clients can download PDF invoices. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Extends the Billing Matter Integration feature on the lawyer side. Spring Boot /billing/client/{caseId} endpoint returns time entries and invoices filtered by case and client, enforced with @PreAuthorize. React billing component renders itemised breakdown. Invoice PDF served from the existing Apache FOP pipeline. No financial transaction processing — view and download only. |
| **Dependencies** | Billing Matter Integration (Lawyer Feature), Case Management, Two-Layer ACL |

| 8\. Privacy & Data Dashboard (GDPR Portal) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | Clients have a legal right to know what data the firm holds and to request deletion, but no interface supports this. |
| **User Experience** | A Privacy Dashboard page lists every piece of client data held: profile fields, documents, messages, audit entries, and AI query logs. A Submit Deletion Request button initiates a GDPR erasure workflow. The client can track the request status. The lawyer/IT Admin processes the request from the Admin Dashboard. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot /privacy/data-inventory endpoint aggregates data from all tables where client\_id appears. deletion\_request table with status (PENDING, IN\_REVIEW, COMPLETED, DENIED). Completion triggers soft-delete of non-ledger data (ledger entries cannot be erased by design — this is disclosed to the client). A GDPR\_DELETION\_REQUEST audit entry is written to both stores. |
| **Dependencies** | Dual-Store Audit Log, User Administration, Automated Compliance Report Generator (Lawyer) |

| 9\. Real-Time Push Notifications |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | The current polling-only notification model means clients miss time-sensitive updates between polls. |
| **User Experience** | Clients receive instant browser notifications for: new messages from counsel, documents shared with them, hearing reminders (24 hours and 1 hour prior), and lawyer-sent reminders. A notification bell icon in the header shows an unread count badge. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot WebSocket endpoint (/ws/notifications) with STOMP, shared with the annotation feature. Events are pushed on message creation, document share grant, and @Scheduled hearing reminders. React context subscribes to the WebSocket on login, dispatches notifications to a Zustand notification store, and triggers the browser Notifications API. This resolves the known real-time gap in the prototype. |
| **Dependencies** | E2E Messaging, Document Management, Hearing Management, Zustand Auth Store |

| 10\. Case Outcome Archive & Evidence Bundle |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Problem** | On case closure, clients have no permanent, portable record of their case with cryptographic proof of document integrity. |
| **User Experience** | When a case is closed, the client is offered a Download Case Archive button. This produces a password-protected ZIP containing all case documents (client-decrypted, then re-encrypted with a client-chosen passphrase), a case summary PDF, the signed hearing record, and an On-Chain Permanence Certificate — a PDF listing every Fabric tx ID associated with the case, with QR codes linking to the ledger. |
| **Technical Sketch** | Spring Boot /cases/{id}/archive endpoint orchestrates the bundle. All document decryption happens client-side; the browser assembles the ZIP using JSZip. The Permanence Certificate PDF is generated server-side from a Fabric query. Case closure anchored with RecordCaseClosure chaincode function, which seals the case ledger record against further mutation. |
| **Dependencies** | Secure Document Download, Digital Signature Workflow (Lawyer), Fabric Chaincode, Court-Ready PDF Bundle Generator |

# **Section 5 — Architecture Considerations**

Each major new capability introduces specific architectural requirements. The table below summarises those requirements and confirms compatibility with the existing blockchain-first cryptographic design. Features not listed in this section (e.g., TOTP recovery codes, document version history UI, billing integration) require only standard Spring Boot and React additions and introduce no architectural complexity.

| Capability Area | Architectural Note |
| :---- | :---- |
| **AI / RAG Pipeline** | Requires a PostgreSQL pgvector extension for semantic search, a sentence-transformer model for embedding generation, and either an OpenAI API integration or a locally hosted LLM. Critically, the RAG model never decrypts documents server-side: all plaintext is passed from the browser to the inference endpoint within a single authenticated TLS session and is not persisted. No changes to the existing chaincode or cryptographic model are required. The AI sidecar is a stateless Spring Boot — Python FastAPI bridge. |
| **Real-Time (WebSocket / SSE)** | Spring Boot WebSocket support (via spring-boot-starter-websocket) is already compatible with the existing Spring Security JWT model — the handshake endpoint authenticates with the same access token. This resolves the known prototype gap. STOMP messages for annotations are signed with ECDSA at the browser, so recipients can verify authorship without trusting the server. No chaincode changes required. |
| **Cross-Firm Data Room** | This is the architecturally most sensitive addition. External parties are not registered in the firm's Hyperledger Fabric MSP. The implementation uses time-limited session keys derived from invitation tokens, not full MSP identities. Access events are recorded on the existing Fabric ledger by the internal server acting as the MSP identity, with the external party identified by a data\_room\_participant\_id. This preserves the cryptographic audit model without requiring MSP expansion. |
| **Multi-Party Digital Signature Workflow** | Requires a new chaincode function RecordSigningCompletion that accepts an array of {signer\_id, public\_key\_fingerprint, signature\_hex, timestamp} tuples and writes them atomically to the ledger. This is fully compatible with the existing contractapi / CouchDB pattern. The browser-side signing logic reuses the existing WebCrypto ECDSA P-256 implementation. |
| **Predictive ML Pipeline** | The ML inference engine operates exclusively on anonymised, aggregated case metadata (case type, jurisdiction, document count, duration, outcome). No document content or personally identifiable information is ever passed to the model. The model is trained offline on a separate pipeline and deployed as a serialised artefact. GDPR Article 22 (automated decision-making) is addressed by presenting predictions as advisory probabilities, not binding determinations, with a mandatory human-review notice in the UI. |
| **Client-Side Document Redaction** | The redaction tool operates entirely in the browser, consistent with the platform's cryptographic model. The server never receives the pre-redaction plaintext — only the encrypted redacted copy and the encrypted original (already stored). The document\_redaction table records the relationship between the two IPFS CIDs. A new chaincode function RecordRedaction is required to anchor both CIDs with the redacting user's identity. |
| **Secure Video Consultation (WebRTC)** | Requires a TURN/STUN relay server for NAT traversal — this is an infrastructure addition not present in the current prototype. The session encryption key is derived via ECIES from the two participants' existing P-256 key pairs, maintaining the existing cryptographic model. The video stream itself is encrypted by the WebRTC stack using DTLS-SRTP; the ECIES layer secures the signalling channel. |

## **5.1  Chaincode Change Summary**

The following new chaincode functions will be required. All follow the existing Go / contractapi / CouchDB-compatible pattern used by legalcc and require no changes to the existing peer or orderer configuration:

| Chaincode Function | Triggered By | Data Anchored |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **RecordTemplateGeneration** | Smart Contract Template Engine | template\_id, param\_hash, case\_id, timestamp |
| **RecordDeadline** | Deadline & SoL Tracker | deadline\_id, case\_id, deadline\_date, type |
| **RecordDataRoomAccess** | Cross-Firm Data Room | data\_room\_id, participant\_id, action, timestamp |
| **RecordSigningCompletion** | Digital Signature Workflow | workflow\_id, \[{signer\_id, pubkey\_fp, sig\_hex, ts}\] |
| **RecordRedaction** | Document Redaction Tool | original\_cid, redacted\_cid, redacting\_user\_id |
| **RecordConsultation** | Secure Video Consultation Booking | consultation\_id, case\_id, participants, datetime |
| **RecordMilestone** | Case Progress Timeline | milestone\_id, case\_id, type, status, timestamp |
| **RecordSettlementResponse** | Settlement Offer Comparison | offer\_id, case\_id, client\_id, response, timestamp |
| **RecordClientSignature** | Client-Side Document Signing | doc\_id, client\_id, signature\_hex, doc\_hash |
| **RecordCaseClosure** | Case Outcome Archive | case\_id, closure\_timestamp, archiving\_user\_id |

# **Section 6 — Development Priority Matrix**

The table below ranks all twenty-seven proposed features across both sides of the platform. Sprint 1 features represent the lowest effort, highest impact improvements that can be delivered rapidly without architectural additions. Sprint 2 features require moderate effort and introduce new data models or integrations. Sprint 3 features require substantial engineering effort but deliver the platform's most differentiating capabilities. Backlog features require significant architectural additions (AI infrastructure, WebRTC relay, external MSP federation) and are planned for later phases.

| Feature | Side | User Impact | Dev Effort | Academic Novelty | Sprint |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| TOTP Recovery Codes | Lawyer | **High** | **Low** | Low | **Sprint 1** |
| Document Version History UI | Lawyer | **High** | **Low** | Low | **Sprint 1** |
| Client-Side Document Signing | Client | **High** | **Low** | High | **Sprint 1** |
| Conflict of Interest Checker | Lawyer | **High** | **Low** | Medium | **Sprint 1** |
| Case Progress Timeline | Client | **High** | **Medium** | Medium | **Sprint 1** |
| Deadline & SoL Tracker | Lawyer | **High** | **Medium** | Medium | **Sprint 1** |
| Real-Time Push Notifications | Client | **High** | **Medium** | Medium | **Sprint 1** |
| AI Document Classification | Lawyer | **High** | **Medium** | High | **Sprint 2** |
| Audit Anomaly Detection | Lawyer | **High** | **Medium** | High | **Sprint 2** |
| Billing Matter Integration | Lawyer | **High** | **Medium** | Low | **Sprint 2** |
| Chain of Custody Visualiser | Lawyer | **High** | **Medium** | High | **Sprint 2** |
| Expense & Invoice Portal | Client | **High** | **Low** | Low | **Sprint 2** |
| Privacy & GDPR Dashboard | Client | **High** | **Medium** | High | **Sprint 2** |
| Client Satisfaction Feedback | Client | **Medium** | **Low** | Low | **Sprint 2** |
| Smart Contract Template Engine | Lawyer | **High** | **Medium** | High | **Sprint 3** |
| Compliance Report Generator | Lawyer | **High** | **Medium** | High | **Sprint 3** |
| Document Redaction Tool | Lawyer | **High** | **High** | High | **Sprint 3** |
| Settlement Offer Comparison | Client | **High** | **Medium** | High | **Sprint 3** |
| Case Outcome Archive | Client | **High** | **Medium** | High | **Sprint 3** |
| Multi-Party Signature Workflow | Lawyer | **High** | **High** | High | **Sprint 3** |
| Court-Ready PDF Bundle | Lawyer | **High** | **High** | Medium | **Sprint 3** |
| Collaborative Annotation | Lawyer | **Medium** | **High** | High | **Sprint 3** |
| RAG Legal AI Assistant | Lawyer | **High** | **High** | High | **Backlog** |
| AI Client Case Chatbot | Client | **High** | **High** | High | **Backlog** |
| Predictive Outcome Analysis | Lawyer | **Medium** | **High** | High | **Backlog** |
| Cross-Firm Data Room | Lawyer | **High** | **High** | High | **Backlog** |
| Secure Video Consultation | Client | **Medium** | **High** | High | **Backlog** |

## **6.1  Sprint Rationale**

Sprint 1 is deliberately weighted towards features that resolve known prototype gaps (TOTP recovery codes, document version UI, real-time notifications) and features that require only the existing cryptographic stack with minimal new engineering (client-side document signing). These deliver immediate demonstrable value with low regression risk.

Sprint 2 introduces the platform's compliance and analytics capabilities, which are directly relevant to the academic assessment context: the audit anomaly detection engine and the chain of custody visualiser both demonstrate sophisticated use of the dual-store audit architecture, while the GDPR privacy dashboard demonstrates responsible data governance design.

Sprint 3 delivers the platform's flagship legal workflow features: the multi-party signature workflow, the redaction tool, and the smart contract template engine. These are the features most likely to differentiate PangoChain in a commercial context and to generate the most substantive academic discussion.

Backlog features — particularly the RAG AI assistant, the cross-firm data room, and the predictive outcome analyser — are architecturally the most significant additions. They require careful infrastructure planning and are best tackled once the Sprint 1–3 foundation is stable.
